main_summary.summary: Timothy Jenkins, born December 30, 1938 in Philadelphia, was interviewed December 9, 2015 at the Library of Congress by Emilye Crosby. Jenkins entered Howard University in 1956 on a full-tuition scholarship and served as student body president; he studied under Rayford Logan, Sterling Brown, E. Franklin Frazier, and Harold Lewis, and identified Howard President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson as a mentor who refused to expel faculty or students targeted by McCarthy-era investigations. After graduating in June 1960, Jenkins was elected National Affairs Vice President of the United States National Student Association (USNSA) for the 1960-61 term and simultaneously sat on the executive committees of SNCC and Students for a Democratic Society. He convened the August 1961 Nashville seminar at Fisk's Race Relations Institute - which he calls the first Freedom School - to ground SNCC field leaders in Black history, constitutional law, and political strategy. He aligned with Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, and Charles Jones in arguing for voter registration over direct action at the Capahosic, Virginia meeting with Kennedy administration aides Harris Wofford and Burke Marshall. Jenkins enrolled at Yale Law School in fall 1961 and helped draft the three-judge-panel amendment to the Voting Rights Act. He later co-founded the National Conference of Black Lawyers (1968), was appointed by President Jimmy Carter to the U.S. Postal Service Board of Governors, served as president of the University of the District of Columbia, and helped establish WHUT-TV at Howard.
key_themes: SNCC voter-registration wing vs religious direct-action wing
USNSA and CIA-funded international student arm
Post-movement institutional infiltration as continuation of struggle
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jenkins offers a first-person account of SNCC's foundational secular-political wing - the strand that argued voter registration and capture of state organs was prerequisite to integration's substantive gains, in tension with the Nashville religious-nonviolence contingent led by Jim Lawson and Diane Nash. He documents the August 1961 Capahosic, Virginia meeting where SNCC representatives Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, Charles Jones, and Jenkins met with Harris Wofford and Burke Marshall; Jenkins explicitly contests Howard Zinn's framing of this as Kennedy-administration co-optation, characterizing the dynamic instead as parallel-interest convergence. He provides a detailed account of co-drafting (with Yale Law classmate Bill Wood) the three-judge-panel provision that made Voting Rights Act enforcement viable in the Fifth Circuit, and identifies Mississippi attorney William L. Higgs as the guiding spirit on that amendment.

The interview also preserves rarely-recorded episodes: Ella Baker's near-fatal Cumberland Mountains accident en route from Highlander to Chattanooga and her deathbed utterance of the grandmother's idiom "Thank God for Jesus"; Jenkins's role on a Jackson rooftop after Medgar Evers's funeral preventing armed Black veterans from shooting John Doar; and his account of Mordecai Wyatt Johnson's influence on King's adoption of Gandhian nonviolence (a lineage Jenkins says is typically attributed to Thoreau and Tillich but which King himself credited to Johnson). Jenkins offers a structural critique of the post-Black-Power period as a failure to extend coalition to poor white communities, which he identifies as a root of contemporary red-state political alignment.

chapters.title: Philadelphia Origins and Grandparents
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes being born in Philadelphia on December 30, 1938 to long-term Philadelphia parents. His maternal grandfather William Albert Jones served on the Philadelphia school board and founded an organization for Black Republicans called Remember John Brown. His paternal grandfather Rev. O.G. Jenkins founded the Courtland Normal and Industrial Institute, a Rosenwald school in Courtland, Virginia (formerly New Jerusalem, renamed after the Nat Turner Rebellion), and built Hood Memorial Church in Richmond.
related_events: Booker T. Washington Tuskegee Institute network

chapters.title: Philadelphia Schooling
chapters.summary: Jenkins attended the Thaddeus Stevens School of Practice on Spring Garden Street, a heavily integrated teacher-training school with Chinese, Eastern European, Jewish, and Irish students. Field trips to the Philadelphia Museum of Art, the Franklin Institute, and the Natural History Museum shaped his aesthetic sensibilities. He then attended Central High School, Philadelphia's premier public institution, where Howard University recruiters distributed scholarship literature.
related_events: Philadelphia Museum of Art Scholars Room visits
Howard University recruitment of Northern students

chapters.title: Howard Faculty and the Little Forum
chapters.summary: Jenkins entered Howard in 1956 on a full-tuition scholarship. He studied under Rayford Logan (Pan-African Movement general secretary under Du Bois), Sterling Brown (literary critic and dialect scholar), Harold Lewis (political scientist), and E. Franklin Frazier (sociologist of the Black bourgeoisie). The Little Forum was a monthly student-faculty reading club in Cook Hall where students and faculty addressed each other as peers - Jenkins identifies it as the most important feature of his Howard education.
related_events: Pan-African Movement under W.E.B. Du Bois
Howard's correspondence-school theology tradition (Sterling Nelson Brown)

chapters.title: Mordecai Johnson and McCarthy-Era Howard
chapters.summary: Jenkins served as Howard student body president and developed a mentor relationship with President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson by waylaying him on the granite bench behind Founders Library. Johnson refused to expel faculty pursued by the House Un-American Activities Committee or to expel Pauli Murray after her 1940s House cafeteria sit-in. Jenkins cites Jonathan Holloway's history of Howard's faculty fervor in the 1940s and 1950s.
related_events: Pauli Murray 1940s U.S. House cafeteria sit-in
House Un-American Activities Committee Howard faculty investigations

chapters.title: Howard Activism and the 1960 Sit-ins
chapters.summary: Howard students protested for Black studies, younger faculty, and curricular reform. When the 1960 sit-ins erupted, Jenkins led the student council in appropriating funds to support arrested students; Dean Armour J. Blackburn forbade direct expenditure, so the council routed money through a Raleigh convention. Jenkins appealed over Blackburn to President Mordecai Johnson, who overruled the dean. He notes NAACP youth chapters - including Barbara Posey's Oklahoma City group - had been sitting in long before Greensboro 1960.

chapters.title: USNSA and the CIA
chapters.summary: Jenkins was elected USNSA National Affairs Vice President for 1960-61 by acclamation - a maneuver by USNSA staff with CIA ties who blocked his preferred candidacy for International Affairs VP because they could not trust him with the confidentiality agreement between the USNSA International Affairs office and the CIA. The International Student Relations Seminar at Harvard, which Jenkins had attended, was CIA-funded though not disclosed at the time. He notes the National Affairs role gave him more influence during the sit-in explosion than International Affairs would have.

chapters.title: Buckley Censure Fight and YAF
chapters.summary: During Jenkins's USNSA term, the young Americans for Freedom invited William F. Buckley Jr. to speak at a parallel meeting adjacent to the USNSA Congress. After Buckley spoke, Jenkins delivered a rebuttal accusing him of "slave-owning fascist mentality." YAF introduced a motion to censure Jenkins for unauthorized political speech. The censure vote was defeated and Jenkins continued in office. He frames the YAF as the early collegiate emergence of the John Birch Society and connects its rhetoric to later anti-abortion and Trump-era political movements.

chapters.title: Ella Baker and the SNCC Executive Committee
chapters.summary: Jenkins met Ella Baker through SNCC. He notes her early association with A. Philip Randolph's Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, her founding role at SCLC, and her work through the Atlanta YWCA office. Reviewing old minutes, Jenkins found he had chaired a December SNCC executive committee meeting at the Butler Street YMCA in Atlanta at which Marion Barry was succeeded as chairman by Chuck McDew. Baker mediated the secular-political vs religious-nonviolent factional split.
related_events: SNCC December executive committee meeting Atlanta
Marion Barry replaced by Chuck McDew as SNCC chairman

chapters.title: SNCC's Secular-Political Wing
chapters.summary: Jenkins identifies a fundamental division in SNCC between the Nashville religious-nonviolence contingent (Jim Lawson, Diane Nash, Jim Bevel, Marion Barry) and a secular-political wing represented by Jenkins, Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, and Charles Jones. The Nashville group insisted "Nonviolent" be added to SNCC's name to distinguish it from SDS. Jenkins's wing argued that direct action without political power was cosmetic - that voting was the only means to control the organs of state determining police, paving, school budgets, and public employment.

chapters.title: Capahosic Meeting with Kennedy Aides
chapters.summary: At Capahosic, Virginia, SNCC representatives Jenkins, Charles Jones, Charles Sherrod, and Chuck McDew met with Harris Wofford (special assistant to President Kennedy) and Burke Marshall (assistant attorney general for civil rights designate) to coordinate a comprehensive South-wide voter education campaign. Jenkins contests Howard Zinn's framing of this as Kennedy administration co-optation - he characterizes the alignment as parallel interest, with SNCC seeking enforcement leverage and the administration seeking orderly political protest over volatile street demonstrations.

chapters.title: Three-Judge Panel and Draft Resistance
chapters.summary: Local Mississippi draft boards targeted SNCC activists. The National Conference of Black Lawyers successfully challenged one case where Gen. Lewis B. Hershey's administration imprisoned a SNCC activist over the statutory 23-year age limit. Jenkins, with Bill Wood at Yale Law, helped force inclusion of a three-judge-panel provision in the Voting Rights Act so voting cases would be heard by panels appointed by the Fifth Circuit chief judge - mostly Eisenhower-appointed Republicans - rather than single segregationist judges appointed via Sen. James O. Eastland's Judiciary Committee. Chairman Emanuel Celler and House Republicans carried the vote.
keywords: Gen. Lewis B. Hershey draft board
related_events: Voting Rights Act House Judiciary Committee testimony

chapters.title: Nashville Seminar - The First Freedom School
chapters.summary: In summer 1961 Jenkins organized a Nashville seminar at Fisk University's Race Relations Institute for SNCC field leaders - what he calls the first Freedom School. Funded by the New World Foundation and the Taconic Foundation with possible Ford support, it brought sit-in leaders together with senior scholars on Black history, politics, economics, and sociology. Dr. King and Ella Baker both wrote letters of support. Stokely Carmichael's memoir Ready for Revolution credits the seminar; Carmichael was reprimanded during the seminar for participating in a downtown Nashville demonstration without strict nonviolence.

chapters.title: Ella Baker and the Cumberland Mountains Accident
chapters.summary: Returning from Highlander Folk School in Monteagle, Tennessee toward the Chattanooga airport, Jenkins and Ella Baker were in a Volkswagen bus when a tractor-trailer driver, seeing the integrated group, ran them off the highway in the Cumberland Mountains. The bus rocked on the edge of a ravine, held by a single tree, until a pickup truck winched them out. As Baker - usually skeptical of religious idioms - was carried to the ambulance on a stretcher, she motioned Jenkins close and said "Thank God for Jesus," the grandmother's idiom she had ridiculed her whole life.

chapters.title: Yale Law and the SNCC Insurance Strategy
chapters.summary: Jenkins enrolled at Yale Law School in fall 1961 for three reasons: legal skills relevant to social change; a SNCC contingency plan to preserve organizational leadership against the risk that field staff might be wiped out by murder, incarceration, or the draft; and to mobilize Northern white campuses as Freedom Singer concert tour hosts and volunteer pipelines. He references himself as "a Che Guevara in the Sierra Maestra" who could re-establish SNCC if it were destroyed. At Yale he worked with Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. and met Peter and Joan Countryman, founders of the Northern Student Movement.

chapters.title: McComb, Mississippi - SNCC Beachhead
chapters.summary: Jenkins explains why SNCC chose McComb: NAACP wanted Atlanta, SCLC wanted Montgomery, CORE wanted Bogalusa and New Orleans, but no one wanted McComb. SNCC went into the most hostile, remote area as a model beachhead. Ella Baker's June 21, 1961 letter introduced Jenkins to Bob Moses and Amzie Moore. The local Black Masonic lodge in McComb gave the project organizing space white police would not enter. Jenkins recounts driving with Chuck McDew, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones from Chicago to McComb through Dial, Alabama, where they hit a dog and a white linen-suited elder dispersed a hostile crowd by saying the boys couldn't have avoided it.

chapters.title: Herbert Lee, Bob Moses, and Teaching for Change
chapters.summary: Jenkins references a WBAI recording of a speech he gave in Holmes County in 1962 or 1963 in which the Herbert Lee murder was the focal point - voter registration would not let Lee die in vain. He describes Bob Moses as a "saintly" intellectual guerrilla and credits Moses's grounded organizing as the model for the Teaching for Change organization, which connects the legacy of the McComb project to ongoing public-education work via the Civil Rights Movement Veterans network.
related_events: Holmes County voter registration mass meeting

chapters.title: Greenwood and the Folk Festival
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes leading singing at the Greenwood Folk Festival on Mrs. McGhee's farm with Bob Dylan, Pete Seeger, and Theo Bikel - the only time he sang with them. Greenwood was a triumph: Mrs. McGhee defended her farm with her armed sons; even residents who couldn't pass the literacy test became defenders and drivers for those who could. Dick Gregory helped secure food supplies when the county cut off food stamps. The county's elected officials are now majority Black as a result.
keywords: Bob Dylan Pete Seeger Theo Bikel
related_events: food stamp retaliation and Dick Gregory food shipments

chapters.title: Albany and Southwest Georgia
chapters.summary: Charles Sherrod chose Southwest Georgia - the highest-Black-population part of the state, neglected while attention went to Atlanta - and built a grassroots Albany movement consistent with Ella Baker's organizing philosophy. Laurie Pritchett's police force, Jenkins argues, was more vicious than Bull Connor's in Birmingham, surrounding marchers in churches and using mob containment. King and SCLC ultimately joined. Jenkins disputes the historians' verdict that Albany failed: he calls it "a slow-boiling success" - residents secured Farmers Home support, learned to navigate USDA forms, and Charles Sherrod's wife Shirley later became a USDA administrator for the Albany region.

chapters.title: Freedom Singers and the Power of Song
chapters.summary: Jenkins traces SNCC's musical tradition to the Fisk Jubilee Singers and the Howard Gospel Singers. He identifies "We Shall Overcome" as Charles Albert Tindley's Methodist hymn from his Philadelphia hometown, turned into a political anthem by Guy Carawan, Pete Seeger, and the Freedom Singers. Song reinforced solidarity among demonstrators in jail and connected the movement to the religious-vocal traditions of the Black community. SNCC veterans still tear up at those songs because of how deeply they index movement history.

chapters.title: Medgar Evers Funeral and Stopping the Rooftop Shooters
chapters.summary: After Medgar Evers's June 1963 assassination, the Connecticut chapter of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom raised funds (channeled through William Sloane Coffin Jr.) for Jenkins to travel to Jackson and be the first person out of state to deliver a check to Myrlie Evers. At the post-funeral march on June 15, 1963, when the youth and SNCC contingent broke from the police-escorted procession to sing freedom songs, Jenkins was alerted that armed Black veterans were on a rooftop with telescopic rifles trained on John Doar - they couldn't hear Doar's words and thought he was acting for the local police. Jenkins reached the roof and shouted "Stop in the name of Medgar." They stopped.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

chapters.title: Atlanta Rich's Sit-In and the 1960 Election
chapters.summary: Jenkins identifies a second "brush with history": at a Morehouse chapel meeting before a planned Rich's department store sit-in in Atlanta, the Black establishment - including Martin Luther King Sr. - opposed direct action in Atlanta. Jenkins urged the group to march to MLK Jr.'s house and ask him to join. King did join, was arrested two days before the Kennedy-Nixon election, and Robert F. Kennedy made his famous call to Coretta. United Auto Workers leaflets distributed the story in major cities. Jenkins states that 250,000 previously non-voting Black voters voted for Kennedy, who won by 100,000.

chapters.title: MFDP, Bill Higgs, and the Voter Education Project
chapters.summary: Lawrence Guyot credits Jenkins with finding the Mississippi statute - originally enacted to protect post-Reconstruction whites from a Republican-controlled legislature - that authorized protest voting and parallel local parties, the legal foundation of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party. Jenkins worked on this with Yale Law classmate Bill Wood. William L. Higgs, an Ole Miss and Harvard Law-trained white attorney who took civil rights cases under Judge William Harold Cox (a Sen. James O. Eastland appointee), was the guiding spirit on the three-judge-panel amendment. The Voter Education Project, headed by Leo Branton and then Vernon E. Jordan Jr., refused to operate in Mississippi - making SNCC's McComb and Greenwood work all the more critical.

chapters.title: Mordecai Johnson, King, and the Gandhian Lineage
chapters.summary: Jenkins cites Larry E. Rivers's article in the Journal of African American History documenting that King heard Mordecai Wyatt Johnson speak in Philadelphia while at Crozer Theological Seminary and locked himself in his room for ten days reading Gandhi afterward. Johnson's vice president William Stuart Nelson had lived in India with Gandhi. Jenkins says King's adoption of nonviolence is typically attributed to Thoreau, Emerson, Carlyle, and Tillich, but King himself credited Johnson - a credit Jenkins says has been distorted and lost in subsequent histories.

chapters.title: National Conference of Black Lawyers
chapters.summary: Jenkins co-founded the National Conference of Black Lawyers in 1968 on a call from Floyd B. McKissick to move the Black bar beyond Brown v. Board of Education into federal regulation, economic development, poverty, and housing law. NCBL members included Judge Leon Higginbotham (Third Circuit), Nathaniel R. Jones, Hon. Bruce Wright, and Raymond Pace Alexander (counsel in the Girard College case establishing no discrimination in charitably funded institutions). NCBL helped defeat Haynsworth and Carswell Supreme Court nominations. Jenkins frames it as the invisible institutional continuation of SNCC.
related_events: Haynsworth and Carswell Supreme Court nomination defeats

chapters.title: Marion Barry, Postal Service, and Post-Movement Career
chapters.summary: Jenkins traces SNCC's continued presence in Washington D.C. through Marion Barry. He served as president of the University of the District of Columbia, was a professor at Howard Law School and David A. Clarke School of Law, and was appointed by President Jimmy Carter (Senate-confirmed) to the U.S. Postal Service Board of Governors, where he chaired the Corporate Responsibility Committee, brought Kenneth B. Clark in to study systemic discrimination, and restored Amzie Moore's pension. He helped establish WHUT-TV at Howard - the only full commercial station owned by an HBCU - and consulted to the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights on the information-highway access gap.
related_events: Kenneth B. Clark Postal Service study

chapters.title: Critique of Black Power and the Unfinished Coalition
chapters.summary: Jenkins wrote John Lewis at the end of SNCC urging that veterans professionally infiltrate EEOC, the National Labor Relations Board, and the Federal Reserve System - the regulatory machinery created by the legislation SNCC helped draft. He argues that SNCC failed to do what labor had done: place its people in administrative roles after legislative victories. He delivers a structural critique: the Black Power era's exclusion of progressive white allies broke the coalition needed to mobilize poor whites - producing today's red-state alignment in which Kentuckians vote against the Affordable Care Act to avoid association with Obama. He references the 1940s Southern Negro Youth Congress (the original SNCC influenced by Du Bois and Eleanor Roosevelt) as a model the next generation must extend.
